summary: Cathy O'Neil highlights Sir Andrew Wiles' criticism of mathematics being misused in the 2008 financial crisis, particularly through complex derivatives manipulation by banks. Wiles argued that math's reputation has been tarnished and that the field has drifted 'towards goals that you might not believe in.' O'Neil celebrates hearing a prominent mathematician acknowledge these ethical concerns. She notes the irony that the building named after Wiles was funded by Landon Clay, whose wealth came from investment management—precisely the industry Wiles was criticizing.
key_points: Wiles publicly stated that mathematics was abused during the financial meltdown through banks' manipulation of complex derivatives
He called for mathematicians to 'protect the good name' of their discipline from misuse
O'Neil sees this as validation for her proposed panel to investigate public math models
The Andrew Wiles Building was funded by an investment management CEO, creating an ironic tension with Wiles' critique
Wealth management tools fall squarely into what O'Neil calls 'weapons of math destruction'
money_quotes: One has to be aware now that mathematics can be misused and that we have to protect its good name.
Mathematics research, which used to be removed from day-to-day life, has diverged 'towards goals that you might not believe in'.
The abuse of mathematics during the global financial meltdown in 2009, particularly by banks' manipulation of complex derivatives, had tarnished his chosen subject's reputation.
Scientists used to worry about the ethical repercussions of their work.
In my experience, wealth management tools are definitely in the 'weapons of math destruction' toolbox.
key_insight: When one of the world's most celebrated pure mathematicians acknowledges that math is being weaponized for profit, it validates growing concerns about the ethical responsibilities of the mathematical community.
